games.explanation: Griffin Canning is the kind of pitcher who keeps getting priced like a problem solver and keeps walking the leadoff man instead. The Nationals run out a lefty-heavy top of the order against an arm that has coughed up a .348 wOBA to left-handed bats and walks them at a double-digit clip, which is exactly the recipe James Wood eats for breakfast. Littell does not miss bats but he does not hand out free passes either, and a Petco game between two middling rotations should stay close enough for the dog price to matter. Getting plus money on the better team is the entire pitch, and +112 is generous for a coin flip the market has tilted the wrong way.

Detroit is running out Keider Montero, a righty whose splits read like a confession: he handles right-handed bats fine and gets dismantled by lefties, which is unfortunate when the White Sox can roll Benintendi, Vargas, and a Montgomery bat that does real damage. The market sees a last-place team on the road and reflexively flips the favorite tag, ignoring that Chicago is actually the stronger side here by a wide Elo margin. Montero has surrendered a .379 xwOBA to left-handed hitters this year, and the projected score lands inside a run, which is not the kind of game where -130 should feel generous. Burke walks his share but the lineup behind him plays the matchup that matters. You're getting the better team at a price the board built for the wrong reasons.

Wacha is the kind of contact-managing righty who looks fine in the box score and then quietly gets stung the second a lineup with real thump shows up, and Texas has enough of that to make the price wrong. Burger and Pederson barrel the ball at rates that punish exactly this profile, and Wacha's xwOBA-against righties sits above where his ERA wants you to believe. Leiter walks too many to love, but a closed roof neutralizes the park's homer tilt and the sim sees a tight one-run game the market is treating as a clearer favorite for Kansas City than it should be. At -116 on the better team by a meaningful Elo gap, you're getting near coin-flip juice on a side that projects above 59%.

picks.explanation: Griffin Canning is the kind of pitcher who keeps getting priced like a problem solver and keeps walking the leadoff man instead. Getting plus money on the better team is the entire pitch, and +112 is generous for a coin flip the market has tilted the wrong way.

Tampa is the better team and Kochanowicz is the worse pitcher, both of which the market understands, but the run line at this price is asking whether the Rays cruise or grind, and a one-run game is the far likelier shape. Even the ugliest version of this Angels start projects out to a 4-4 type fight, with the sim landing inside a single run and the park giving nobody a tailwind. Trout against righties remains a real problem in a short series of swings, and the Rays lineup, for all its quality, is more grind-it-out than crooked-number, sitting closer to a .370 xwOBA at the top than anything truly scary. Laying -120 on a cover when the cover hits closer to 64% is the kind of arithmetic that doesn't need dressing up.

Burger and Pederson barrel the ball at rates that punish exactly this profile, and Wacha's xwOBA-against righties sits above where his ERA wants you to believe. The Yankees being favored by this much assumes Will Warren is going to do something he has not actually done all year, which is shut down a lefty-heavy lineup. Warren's splits against left-handed bats are ugly enough that Nick Kurtz alone justifies a second look, and Jacob Lopez quietly runs better contact-suppression numbers than the guy he's opposing. The sim has this landing inside a run, which is what happens when the better-rated starter wears the worse uniform. Getting a buck and a half with Kurtz teed up against the exact arm profile he eats for breakfast is the kind of price the market hands out when it's looking at logos instead of matchups.

Two near-identical teams on the Elo sheet shouldn't be priced as a coin flip plus a full run of cushion, but here we are. Bryce Miller has been getting barreled up by everyone with a pulse this year, and Arizona happens to run out a lineup where Marte, Carroll, and Perdomo all post xwOBAs north of .350 against righties. Merrill Kelly on the other side is the steadier arm by a comfortable margin, which is exactly the profile you want when you're buying insurance runs in a pitcher-friendly park with the roof shut. The sim spits out a one-run game more often than not, and -170 to stay within that one run is the cleanest read on the board.
